coredata.dc:title: Highly enhancing chloride immobilization of cement pastes by novel polymer dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Zhong, An-Nan
Zheng, Shu-Yi
Guo, Hong-Ru
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Chloride-caused corrosion of reinforcing steel is a dominant factor in the durability degradation of reinforced concrete (RC) structures. Nevertheless, there remains a deficiency in suitable additives to augment the resistance of RC structures against chloride-induced corrosion. In light of this, a novel nanomaterial (i.e., polymer dots (PDs)) with high dispersity and low cost is utilized for the first time to amplify the chloride immobilization of cement pastes. And the corresponding mechanism is disclosed in depth. Specifically, PDs are prepared at ambient temperature based on Schiff base reaction and exhibit excellent long-term dispersion stability in simulated concrete pore solution. The incorporation of 0.2 wt% PDs sharply enhance the chloride immobilization of hardened cement pastes by 106 % after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution (3 mol/L). Meanwhile, the water adsorption rate of the hardened cement paste with 0.2 wt% PDs is effectively reduced by 11.5 %. More importantly, depending on the analyses of phase compositions and microstructures of PDs-incorporated cement pastes, the mechanism of PDs-induced chloride immobilization improvement is rationally ascribed to the synchronized enhancement of the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding, as well as the augmentation of chloride migration resistance within cement matrices. This work introduces a newfangled nanomaterial to considerably promote the chloride immobilization of cement pastes, expected to enhance the chloride corrosion resistance of RC structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chloride immobilization
Cement paste
Polymer dots
Phase composition
Microstructure
Mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water adsorption of diverse cement pastes
Phase compositions and microstructures of diverse cement pastes
Mechanism of PDs enhancing chloride immobilization of cement pastes

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of PDs: (a) TEM (including size distribution of PDs inset) and (b) HRTEM images; (c) AFM image and (d) the height profile along the white line within the AFM image.
Structural properties and chemical composition of PDs (a) Raman spectra; (b) FTIR spectra; (c) full-range XPS spectra; high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s.
(a) UV–vis spectra (inset with photographs PDs aqueous solution in sunlight and under 365 nm UV light) of PDs; (b) PL spectra of PDs.

(a) Photographs of PDs-containing saturated CH solution under daylight and a 365 nm UV light; (b) UV–vis spectra and (c) PL spectra of PDs-containing saturated CH solutions; (d) UV–vis spectra and (e) PL spectra of PDs-incorporated saturated CH solutions prior to and following 7-day standing.
(a) Immobilized chloride contents and (b) increased rates of immobilized chloride contents from diverse PDs-incorporated paste samples at diverse immersion times in NaCl solution.
The W a of various PDs-adding paste samples after 28-day curing.
XRD spectrum of various paste sample (a) prior to and (b) following immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d.
(a) DTG of different paste samples following 14 days of submersion in NaCl solution; (b) total immobilized chloride and distribution of immobilized chlorides within Fs and C–S–H.
SEM images of various paste samples following immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d: (a) P0, (b) P1, (c) P2; (d) P3 and (e) P4.
MIP results of different groups after 28-day curing: (a) cumulative porosity; (b) pore size distribution.
Schematic for the mechanism of PDs enhancing chloride immobilization of cement paste.
Phase contents of various paste samples based on the XRD-Rietveld approach after immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d (wt%).
Porosity and pore size classification determined by MIP.

head.ce:title: Highly enhancing chloride immobilization of cement pastes by novel polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Taizhou University, Taizhou, Zhejiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Taizhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Taizhou University, Taizhou, Zhejiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, Guangdong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chloride-caused corrosion of reinforcing steel is a dominant factor in the durability degradation of reinforced concrete (RC) structures. Nevertheless, there remains a deficiency in suitable additives to augment the resistance of RC structures against chloride-induced corrosion. In light of this, a novel nanomaterial (i.e., polymer dots (PDs)) with high dispersity and low cost is utilized for the first time to amplify the chloride immobilization of cement pastes. And the corresponding mechanism is disclosed in depth. Specifically, PDs are prepared at ambient temperature based on Schiff base reaction and exhibit excellent long-term dispersion stability in simulated concrete pore solution. The incorporation of 0.2 wt% PDs sharply enhance the chloride immobilization of hardened cement pastes by 106 % after 14-day immersion in NaCl solution (3 mol/L). Meanwhile, the water adsorption rate of the hardened cement paste with 0.2 wt% PDs is effectively reduced by 11.5 %. More importantly, depending on the analyses of phase compositions and microstructures of PDs-incorporated cement pastes, the mechanism of PDs-induced chloride immobilization improvement is rationally ascribed to the synchronized enhancement of the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding, as well as the augmentation of chloride migration resistance within cement matrices. This work introduces a newfangled nanomaterial to considerably promote the chloride immobilization of cement pastes, expected to enhance the chloride corrosion resistance of RC structures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Reinforced concrete (RC) structures are progressively prevalent in several large-scale infrastructures, and their durability is a hot issue, thus attracting intensive attention [1]. The durability degradation of RC structures not only presents tremendous safety risk but also brings about incalculable economic losses [2]. Chloride-induced reinforcement corrosion is a predominant factor, especially in coastal settings and deicing salt environments [3,4]. Specifically, free chloride ions within concrete pore solution permeate to the surface of reinforcing steel through the pores, and compromise its passivation layer after surpassing the threshold concentration. Subsequently, the reinforcement undergoes corrosion, followed by rusting and expansion. Ultimately, cracks are formed in concrete, giving rise to its spalling [5,6]. Therefore, the durability and longevity of concrete are drastically jeopardized.

To surmount the aforementioned challenge, a large body of approaches is implemented to enhance the chloride-invasion resistance of concrete, such as corrosion inhibitors [7,8], cathodic protection [9], functional coating [10], and chloride immobilization technique [11,12]. Among them, chloride immobilization technique is considered one of the most efficacious and cost-efficient methods, since it considerably converts free chloride ions into immobilized chloride ions. The chloride immobilization can be delved into three parts: (1) physical adsorption, primarily attributing to the adsorption of free chloride ions to calcium silicate hydrate gels (C–S–H) via electrostatic interaction or van der Waals force [13,14]; (2) chemical binding, predominantly ascribed to the reaction between calcium aluminum hydrate (AFm, C4AXH12, X refers to interlayer anion) phase and chloride ions to generate Friedel’s salt (C4ACl2H10, Fs) or Kuzel’s salt (Ks) [15,16]; (3) migration resistance, mainly blocking the transport pathways by refining the pore structure [11,12]. All in all, this approach fundamentally diminishes the content of free chloride ions within concrete, suppressing them from recurrently deteriorating the passivation layer of reinforcement, thereby greatly enhancing the service life of RC structures.

Nanomaterials possess advantages involving high reactivity, small size, low dosage, and large specific surface area, enabling them to become an effective way for chloride immobilization technique. Specifically, nanomaterials exert nucleation effect within cement matrices to facilitates the hydration process of cement, thus augmenting the production of more C–S–H or AFm phase. Meanwhile, nanomaterials possess filler effect, dramatically refining pore structure of cement matrices, thus impeding chloride ions from transportation. So far, a variety of nanomaterials comprising nano-SiO2 [17] nano-Al2O3 [11,18], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [19,20], and carbon-based nanomaterials [15,21], are applied to boost the chloride immobilization of cementitious materials. For example, Liu et.al [17] investigated the effect of colloidal nano-SiO2 (CNS) on the chloride immobilization of cement pastes. They revealed that incorporating 0.5 wt% CNS promoted the chloride immobilization by 10.86 % after 28-day curing, ascribed to the incremental of C-S-H, thereby improving the chloride physical adsorption and chloride migration resistance. Regarding nano-Al2O3, Tan’s group also elucidated the potential application of nano-Al2O3 in the chloride immobilization[11]. Specifically, they observed that the incorporation of 4.0 wt% nano-Al2O3 led to a notable enhancement of 29 % in the chloride immobilization of cement pastes after 180-day curing. The underlying mechanism was attributed to the enhanced chloride chemical binding through more generation of Fs and the improved chloride migration resistance via the refinement of pore structure. Comparable observations are reported in a recent work, Li’s group [18] substantiated that the addition of nano-Al2O3 not only improved chloride immobilization of cementitious materials but also impeded their ingress, and the optimum dosage of nano-Al2O3 was 3.0 wt%. The related mechanism was assigned to the fact that nano-Al2O3 boosted the quantity of Fs to improve chemical binding, and formed more C–S–H to strengthen physical adsorption. Concurrently, nano-Al2O3 decelerated the chloride transportation and adsorption rate. Apart from nano-Al2O3, LDHs demonstrate promising potential in enhancing chloride immobilization of cement-based materials in recent years. Yazdi et.al [19] conducted a study on the efficacy of MgAl-NO3 LDH on chloride immobilization of cementitious materials. Their observations demonstrated that the incorporation of 10 wt% MgAl-NO3 LDH diminished the concentration of free chloride within cement paste by over 25 %, attributing to the adsorption of LDHs on the free chloride ions. Meanwhile, LDH provided nucleating sites for calcium aluminum silicate hydrate (C–A–S–H) and AFm phase, and diminished the porosity of cement matrices, thereby significantly promoting chloride immobilization of cement pastes. Zuo’s group [20] certified that 2.0 wt% NO2-containing LDHs not only amplified the immobilization of chloride ions but also facilitated the release of corrosion inhibitors (
NO
2
−
), thus efficiently augmenting the corrosion resistance of steel. More recently, carbon-based nanomaterials are emerged to enhance chloride immobilization of cementitious materials. Li discovered that carbon nanotubes (CNTs) enhanced the formation of additional C–S–H, consequently augmenting the chloride physical adsorption within concrete [21]. Moreover, Khayat’s group verified the efficacy of adding graphene oxide (GO) into cement pastes for enhancing chloride immobilization. They revealed that incorporating 0.2 wt% GO brought about a significant 46 % elevation in the chloride immobilization of cement pastes [15]. In summary, although tremendous progress is achieved in existing studies, there are still several intractable problems to be tackled. First, the existing nanomaterials, especially LDHs and GO, are expensive, enormously limiting their actual application. Second, all of the aforementioned nanomaterials exhibit inadequate dispersion and inevitably agglomerate in alkali cement environment. This not only severely impairs their promising effects but also deteriorates the properties of cement (e.g. rheological behavior) [22]. More seriously, poor dispersion of nanomaterials drastically increases the cost due to their compensatory incorporation into cementitious materials [23]. Consequently, it is imperative to develop a well-dispersed and cost-effective nanomaterial to improve the chloride immobilization of cementitious materials.

Exhilaratingly, polymer dots (PDs), as a newfangled family member of carbon-based nanomaterials, receive widespread research and attention due to their merits of favorable dispersity, cost-efficiency, facile preparation, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness [24,25]. Typically, PDs are defined as small-sized, monodisperse carbon nanoparticles with unique fluorescent properties, formed via polymerization, cross-linking, dehydration, and carbonization of functional group-rich organic molecules or polymers [26,27]. PDs consist of highly cross-linked or slightly graphitized hydrophobic carbon core, hydrophilic polymer chain shell, and abundant surface groups [28,29]. These characteristics confer PDs on excellent nucleation effect and filler effect, endowing them with superior potential application in chloride immobilization technology. More importantly, compared with existing nanomaterials, PDs may exhibit stronger enhancement on chloride immobilization of cementitious materials, since they are highly dispersed, adequately exerting their nucleation effect and filler effect. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, there are no studies concerning the application of PDs in chloride immobilization of cementitious materials until now. Hence, it urgently conducts a comprehensive study to assess the feasibility and availability of PDs towards the enhancement of chloride immobilization capacity.

Considering this, this work elaborately prepares novel, and well-dispersed PDs and employs them for the first time to improve the chloride immobilization of cement-based materials. More significantly, the relevant mechanism is reasonably brought forward. Specifically, PDs are fabricated at ambient temperature based on the Schiff base reaction between 1,2-Phenylenediamine (o-PD) and 1,4-Benzoquinone (p-BQ). Their favorable long-term dispersion stability in saturated Ca(OH)2 (CH) solution (i.e. simulated concrete pore solution) is confirmed by optical photographs, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis). The influence of PDs on the chloride immobilization of cement pastes is investigated using chloride adsorption equilibrium test. And the water adsorption property of PDs-containing cement pastes is examined by water adsorption test to further elucidate their impermeability. Moreover, on the basis of the analyses of phase compositions and microstructures from X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP), the mechanism referring to PDs-promoted chloride immobilization is rationally proposed. These findings provide an insight into the promising capabilities of PDs in the chloride immobilization technique, expecting to augment the resistance of RC structures against chloride-induced corrosion.

The reagents for synthesizing PDs including o-PD and p-BQ were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were AR grade and required no additional purification prior to use. All the experiments were executed using deionized (DI) water. The PI 52.5 R Portland cement (PC) was applied in this work, which met the Chinese standards of GB175 and GB176. The chemical composition and physical characteristics of PC were presented in
Table 1 and Table 2

In this study, PDs were synthesized through a facile method based on Schiff base reaction. That is, PDs were synthesized at ambient temperature for 3 h using o-PD and p-BQ as precursors without other reagents. The specific synthesis procedure was shown below: o-PD and p-BQ in a 1:1 mass ratio were combined in deionized water to achieve a uniform mixture, facilitated by ultrasonic agitation for 5 min. Following this, the admixture was agitated continuously for 3 h at 25 °C within a thermostatic incubator shaker. The resultant dark brown solution was subsequently purified through centrifugation and vacuum suction filtration utilizing a 0.2 μm membrane, followed by dialysis employing a 100–500 Da membrane to remove any remaining particulates and unreacted substances. Ultimately, solid brown PDs were obtained after undergoing rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying.

To assess the influence of PDs on the chloride immobilization of cementitious materials, five samples with varying dosages of PDs (by weight of cement) were fabricated and designated as P0, P1, P2, P3, and P4, respectively. The precise compositions of the mixtures were outlined in Table 3. To ensure consistency, a water-cement ratio (w/c) of 0.5 was standardized across all samples. During the preparation of the cement pastes, the corresponding dosage of PDs was dissolved in DI water. Subsequently, the aqueous solution of PDs was mixed with cement. The obtained mixture was cast into a 20 mm ×20 mm ×20 mm plastic mold and subsequently covered with plastic film to inhibit the evaporation of water. When the cement pastes were hardened, the samples were taken out of the molds and transferred into a curing chamber at a temperature of 20±2°C and a relative humidity exceeding 95 % for a 28-day curing.

The morphology of PDs was investigated employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images obtained from FEI Talos F200S and Brucker MFP-3D infinity separately. Fourier infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific IS10), Raman (Thermo Scientific DXR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) spectra were used to characterize the structure information and chemical composition of PDs. UV–vis and PL spectra obtained by PerkinElmer Lambda 750 instrument and Hitachi F7000 separately were used to investigate the optical characteristics of PDs in aqueous solution and saturated CH solution.

The chloride immobilization performance of PDs-containing pastes samples was measured using the chloride adsorption equilibrium method [30]. Following 28-day curing, the samples were dried at 60℃ in an oven to achieve a stable quality. These dried samples were then pulverized to a fine powder, with particle sizes not exceeding 0.75 μm. 4 g of the cement powder were allocated to each specimen, which was put in a 120 mL centrifuge tube filled with 40 mL of a 3 mol/L NaCl solution. To prevent chloride salts from crystallizing due to evaporation, each centrifuge tube was sealed and protected from sunlight and temperature changes. Regular agitation of the tubes was implemented to ensure comprehensive interaction between the cement powder and the chloride. The resultant mixtures were subsequently centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and the liquid supernatant was separated at immersion intervals of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, and 14 d. The supernate was employed to quantify the content of chloride ions, while the remaining sediment was analyzed to identify its phase composition. The quantification of free chloride ion concentration was conducted using an automated potentiometric titrator with a 0.1 mol/L AgNO3 solution. The immobilized chloride content in each sample was caculated by Eq. 1.
(1)
C
I
=
35.453 ×
V
0
× (
C
0
−
C
f
)
W
Where
C
I
(mg/g paste) referred to the immobilized chloride content;
V
0
(mL) denoted the volume of the solution;
C
0
and
C
f
(mg/g paste) signified the primary and ultimate chloride ions content within solution, respectively; W (g) corresponded to the quality of the powder sample that was introduced into NaCl solution.

The content of Fs was determined employing Eq. 2. This calculation relied on the hypothesis that the weight loss of Fs calculated through TGA was associated with the six molecules of primary interlayer water within the crystal structure of Fs [31]. Furthermore, the immobilized chloride content attributable to Fs (C Fs I) was ascertained by Eq. 3, and the quantification of the physically adsorbed chloride (
C
I
CSH
) resulting from C–S–H was conducted using Eq. 4.
(2)
m
Fs

M

(3)

C

Fs
Where
M
Fs
represented the molar mass of Fs, which is 561 g/mol;
m
H
referred to the mass loss of the main interlayer of water within the crystal structure of Fs (wt%);
M
H
denoted the molar mass of water, which is 18 g/mol, and
M
C l
indicated the molar mass of Cl, amounting to 35.5 g/mol. It is significant to acknowledge that a Cl/Al ratio of 1 in Fs represented a simplification for the calculation.

Water-adsorption properties of the cement pastes were assessed through water absorption tests to reveal their permeability characteristics. Samples subjected to 28-day curing were submersed in absolute alcohol for 7 d to stop hydration, followed by desiccation in an oven at 105 °C for 72 h. Immediately following their removal from the oven, the mass of each specimen was recorded (denoted as M 0). Subsequently, these samples underwent a complete submersion in water for 48 h, post which they were removed from the water. The samples were then dried, and a subsequent mass measurement was conducted (referred to as M 1). The degree of water absorption ratio (W a) was quantified employing Eq. 5. To mitigate potential experimental inaccuracies, three specimens from each group were evaluated. The final W a value was obtained by calculating the average W a value of three samples.
(5)
W a
=
M 1
−
M 0
M 0
× 100 %

To elucidate the effect of PDs on the chloride immobilization characteristics of PDs-containing paste samples, a thorough examination was carried out utilizing XRD, TGA, SEM, and MIP. These analyses focused on evaluating the phase composition and microstructure in the specimens prior to and following immersion in NaCl solution. The preparatory steps for the samples involved drying the centrifuged sediment, as detailed in Section 2.5 . XRD spectrum were obtained using D8 Advance manufactured by Brucker, employing a copper target to generate X-rays. The diffractometer was set to a scanning speed of 0.2 °/s, with a working voltage of 40 kV and a working current of 40 mA. For quantitative analysis of the XRD data based on the Rietveld refinement method, each sample was mixed with 20 wt% ZnO and homogenized in an agate mortar. When semi-quantitative analysis by Rietveld refinement method, each result ensured that the goodness of fit value was less than 2. Differential thermogravimetric (DTG) analyses were conducted across 25–1000 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere, utilizing a ramp rate of 10 °C/min. These analyses were performed using STA-409PC manufactured by Netzsch. SEM imaging was executed on Thermo APREO S manufactured by Thermo Scientific to observe the microstructural attributes of the samples. MIP (AutoPore IV 9500 Micromeritic) was employed to evaluate the porosity in paste samples.

A comprehensive characterization of the as-prepared PDs, encompassing morphology, structure, and surface groups is performed. Among them, the morphology of PDs is evaluated by TEM and AFM. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, The TEM image displays the monodispersed and quasi-spherical PDs without apparent agglomeration. By stochastically counting over 120 particles, PDs demonstrate a size distribution ranging from 13.2–29.7 nm, with a mean average particle size of approx. 19.89 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) reveals that PDs have no visible lattice fringe, suggesting that they possess low crystallinity. AFM image (Fig. 1c) further confirms the outstanding monodispersity of PDs, coincidental with the finding in TEM image. Additionally, the topographical height of PDs is within the range of 22–26 nm (Fig. 1d), almost in accordance with their particle size counted in TEM. This further supports the spherical morphology of PDs.

To obtain structural and chemical composition insights into the as-synthesized PDs, Raman spectra, FTIR spectra, and XPS are employed. Raman spectra ( Fig. 2a) exhibit two apparent peaks, the D peak at 1349 cm−1 associated with the disordered sp3 defect and the G peak at 1592 cm−1 corresponding to the sp2 hybridized carbon. The intensity ratio of two peaks (I D/I G) is 1.17, suggesting that the as-obtained PDs have a low degree of crystallinity, agreeing well with TEM. FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b) show several characteristic absorption peaks. The peaks occurring between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds [32], and a less intensity peak at 1766 cm−1 is indicative of CO stretching vibrations [33]. The distinct peak at 1627 cm−1 is referred to the stretching vibration of the CN band, demonstrating the involvement of Schiff base reactions in the synthesis of PDs [34,35]. Additionally, the peaks at 1477, 1371, 1224, and 1105 cm−1 suggest the presence of C–N, –NO2, N–H, and C–O–C bonds [35], respectively. These oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups in PDs greatly enhance their dispersity in aqueous systems. XPS provides detailed chemical composition and surface element valence of PDs. The full-range XPS spectra (Fig. 2c) indicate three obvious peaks at 284, 400, and 532 eV, substantiating the presence of C (76.80 %), N (11.18 %), and O (12.02 %), respectively. Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS C1s spectra (Fig. 2d) presents four binding energy peaks: C–C/CC (284.0 eV), C– N (285.0 eV), C–OH (286.1 eV) and CN/CO (290.1 eV). The N1s spectra is resolved into three distinct peaks belonging to CN (398.7 eV), N–H (400.1 eV), and –NO2 (405.4 eV), respectively [35]. Furthermore, the O1s spectra (Fig. 2f) identifies two peaks, belonging to CO (530.3 eV) and C–O (532.4 eV). The above observations further corroborate the results of FTIR analysis. Overall, the structural property studies of PDs firmly demonstrate that the as-synthesized PDs own an amorphous lattice structure as well as abundant oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups.

Optical performance is an inherent property of PDs [28].To evaluate the optical characteristics of the as-prepared PDs, UV–vis and PL spectra are utilized. Fig. 3a reveals that PDs aqueous solution exhibits two distinct absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectra, namely an absorption peak at 265 nm (π−π* transition of sp2 carbon) and a slight absorption peak at 318 nm (n−π* transitions of oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups) [36,37]. The PDs aqueous solution displays a yellow color in daylight and exhibits bright orange fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 3a). The PL spectra (Fig. 3b) demonstrate that the PL behavior of PDs is excitation-dependent, and their excitation and emission maximum occur at 400 nm and 514 nm, respectively. The distinctive optical properties of PDs offer a newfangled approach for evaluating their dispersion stability in saturated CH solution (i.e. simulated concrete pore solution).

Overall, the above analyses including morphological, structural, chemical composition, and optical performance of as-prepared PDs solidly demonstrate that they possess the attribute of monodisperse quasi-spherical morphology (∼19.89 nm), low crystallinity, abundant oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups, and distinctive optical properties. These properties may render PDs promising candidates for board applications in chloride immobilization technology.

It is widely known that excellent dispersion stability is a fundamental prerequisite for nanomaterials to efficiently manifest their positive effect on cement matrices [38,39]. As depicted in Fig. 4a, after 7-day standing, no precipitate is observed in saturated CH solutions containing different concentrations of PDs. And as the concentration of PDs elevates, the solutions display a more noticeable yellow color under sunlight. Generally, the PL of PDs is adversely affected and even quenched when they undergo agglomeration (known as aggregation-induced quenching) [8]. Under 365 nm UV exposure, the orange fluorescence of the solutions becomes progressively more intense with PDs concentration increasing. This qualitatively indicates that PDs are highly dispersed without any aggregation in saturated CH solution irrespective of their concentrations, and possess superior long-term dispersion stability. Benefiting from the inherent optical properties of PDs, their dispersion stability in saturated CH solution is verified utilizing UV–vis and PL spectra. Based on Fig. 4b and c, it is evident that the UV–vis and PL spectra of PDs-incorporated saturated CH solution exhibit a progressive increase in peak intensities as the dosage of PDs amplifies. More significantly, their peak intensities are essentially unchanged after 7-day standing (Fig. 4d and e). This quantitatively manifests that PDs maintain favorable long-term dispersion stability within alkaline concrete environments. In brief, both the qualitative and quantitative analyses collectively confirm that PDs display staggering long-term dispersion stability in saturated CH solution, effectively addressing one of the most intractable problems of nanomaterials (i.e., agglomeration) employed in concrete environments. Hence, this is a strong cornerstone for improving chloride immobilization of cementitious materials.

To shed light on the impact of PDs on the chloride immobilization of cementitious materials, the immobilized chloride content (C I) of PDs-containing paste samples is determined after immersion in NaCl solution at different days using the chloride adsorption equilibrium test [30]. Fig. 5a illustrates that the C I values from all paste samples exhibit a notable increase over immersing time. Simultaneously, the incorporation of PDs can effectively improve the C I values at any immersion period. Among them, P4 group (containing 0.2 wt% PDs) demonstrates the greatest C I value of 54.22 mg/g after 14-day immersion, boosting a staggering 106 % compared with the reference group (P0 group). It is widely recognized that the aggregation of nanomaterials within cementitious materials inevitably undermines their enhancing effects. Hence, the aforementioned observation highlights the superior dispersion of PDs in cement pastes. In addition, as depicted in Fig. 5b, after 1-day immersion, all the PDs-adding groups exhibit the greatest increased rates of C I values regardless of PDs dosage. Notably, P4 group demonstrated the maximum increased rate of the C I values, reaching a remarkable 169 %. This signifies that PDs enhance the chloride immobilization of paste samples more pronouncedly during the early immersion period. As the immersion stage extends from 3 to 14 d, there is a noticeable diminishing trend of C I increase rates for PDs-incorporated cement pastes, indicating that the elevation of PDs on chloride immobilization compromises with the increase of the immersion periods. To sum up, the addition of PDs contributes to improving the chloride immobilization of cement pastes. More importantly, PDs exhibit outstanding dispersity and extreme cost-effectiveness, endowing PDs with greater application potential in chloride immobilization technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water adsorption of diverse cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the water adsorption ratio (W a) of cementitious materials is a reflection of their intrinsic porosity and permeability, playing a pivotal role in assessing the chloride migration resistance within cement matrices [40]. Hence, the water adsorption test is conducted on diverse paste samples after 28-day curing. As displayed in Fig. 6, the W a values of PDs-containing paste samples are consistently less than that of the P0 group, and the higher the PDs dosage, the smaller the W a value. Consequently, P4 group exhibits the lowest W a of 23.04 %, occasioning 11.5 % less than the reference group. This manifests that the introduction of highly dispersed PDs is conducive to the formation of more compact cement matrices, effectively preventing the permeation of water (as well as chloride ions) through their pores, thus remarkably enhancing their resistance to chloride attack.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase compositions and microstructures of diverse cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para:
To enhance the understanding of the mechanism behind the chloride immobilization enhancement in PDs-containing cement pastes, the phase composition and microstructure of PDs-incorporated paste samples at different dosages piror to and following immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d are comprehensively investigated by XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP. Fig. 7 displays the XRD spectrum of various paste samples prior to and following 14 days of immersion in NaCl solution. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, distinct diffraction peaks encompassing the unhydrated products C3S (2θ=32.3°) and C2S (2θ=32.7°), hydration product CH (2θ=18.1°, 34° and 47.1°), and the carbonization product CaCO3 (Cc, 2θ=29.4°) are observed in all the paste samples without immersion. This indicates that the incorporation of PDs cannot alter the type of phase within cement pastes. Furthermore, it is widely acknowledged that the intensity of the main diffraction peak originating from CH at 18.1°is directly correlated with its quantity [41,42]. Therefore, a stronger peak at 18.1° demonstrates a greater amount of CH. Obviously, the CH peak at 18.1° is noticeably enhanced with the addition of PDs. Among them, the P4 group (with 0.2 wt% PDs) displays the highest CH peak. This suggests that the incorporation of PDs notably facilitates the hydration process of cement probably attributing to the nucleation effect of PDs conducing to the improvement of chloride immobilization of cementitious materials. After 14-day immersion (Fig. 7b), the peak intensity of CH at 18.1° drastically dwindles in all the paste samples, and new diffraction peaks of Fs (2θ=11°, 22o, and 23°) are observed. Since Fs is generated by the reaction among AFm phase, CH, and chloride ions (as depicted in Eq. 6), Fs-caused chloride chemical binding occurs [43,44]. It is worth noticing that the intensity of CH peak presents a decreasing trend with the increase of PDs dosage, while the peak of Fs at 11.7° intensifies with the dosage of PDs. Among them, P4 group with the highest content of PDs possesses the lowest CH peak and strongest Fs peak. It can be inferred that PDs is conducive to promoting the CH reaction-induced formation of Fs, thereby enhancing the chemical binding performance of chloride ions in cement pastes.
(6) C4AXH12+Ca(OH)2+2Cl-→C4ACl2H10+2OH-+CaX⋅2H2O

To obtain more detailed information regarding phase composition, the XRD-Rietveld method is applied to conduct a quantitative assessment of phase contents within diverse paste samples following 14-day immersion in NaCl solution. As revealed in Table 4, the contents of unhydrated phases (C2S and C3S) in PDs-containing samples evidently decline compared with that of the P0 group. Conversely, the quantity of Fs and C–S–H remarkably elevates, exhibiting a positive proportion to PDs dosage. With respect to P4 group with 0.2 wt% PDs, the contents of C–S–H and Fs are 67.41 wt% and 7.83 wt%, demonstrating 14 % and 33 % more than that of the reference group separately. These aforementioned observations imply that the incorporation of PDs substantially facilitates cement hydration, bringing about more Fs and C–S–H formation. This significantly enhances the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding in PDs-incorporated paste samples, finally augmenting their chloride immobilization. Among them, within the PDs dosage of 0–0.2 wt%, the more the incorporation of PDs, the better the chloride immobilization of paste samples.

DTG is conducted to acquire more precise information on hydration product contents in diverse paste samples. Fig. 8a shows the DTG results for all the samples, differentiating various phases of cement pastes according to their mass losses at distinct temperature intervals. Indeed, all the paste samples possess similar mass loss peaks at certain temperature ranges, attributing to the decomposition of C–S–H/Ettringite (AFt) (90–150 °C), AFm phase (150–200 °C), Fs (280–360 °C), CH (450–500 °C), and Cc (600–800 °C), respectively [45,46]. This further corroborates that the incorporation of PDs fails to alter the composition of hydration products, in conformity with the XRD results. More precisely, the major hydration product C–S–H exhibit a loss of bound water at the range of 50–600 °C, with a notable peak at approximately 100 °C [47]. As for AFt, their crystalline water predominantly undergoes removal at 105–114 °C [48]. Given the mass ratio of C–S–H is significantly greater compared with AFt, the mass loss peak (90–150 °C) is mainly dominated by C–S–H. Generally, the intensity of the mass loss peak is directly proportional to the content of the relevant phase [38,39]. Evidently, the mass loss peaks associated with C–S–H of PDs-containing paste samples are much more pronounced than that of the reference group, and an increased addition of PDs correlates with a higher production of C–S–H. This strongly indicates that the addition of PDs facilitates the enhancement of cement hydration, leading to the formation of a greater quantity of C–S–H, thereby resulting in a substantial improvement in the chloride physical adsorption. Furthermore, compared with the reference group, PDs-modified samples demonstrate significantly increased levels of AFm phase and Fs content. The more the PDs dosage, the more the AFm phase and Fs content, with the P4 group exhibiting the highest concentrations of both. This indicates that PDs are effective in boosting the formation of AFm phase, and AFm phase is capable of transforming into Fs due to ion exchange. Correspondingly, a greater generation of Fs is found in PD-incorporated samples, tremendously enhancing the chemically bound chloride content of cement pastes. This finding aligns with the deductions from XRD. In a word, the qualitative result derived from DTG indicates that incorporating PDs notably enhances both the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding of cementitious materials, thereby markedly promoting their chloride immobilization.

To verify the distribution of immobilized chloride ions within Fs and C–S–H following immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d, the chemically immobilized chloride caused by Fs (C Fs I) is quantified by Eq. 3. Subsequently, the content of physically adsorbed chloride attributed to C–S–H (C CSH I) is determined through Eq.4. The calculation result is depicted in Fig. 8b. Undoubtfully, the introduction PDs tremendously boosts both C CSH I and C Fs I values, and enhancement is directly proportionate to the quantity of PDs dosage. Consequently, the P4 group records the greatest C CSH I and C Fs I values at 28.06 mg/g and 26.16 mg/g, representing an increase of 266 % and 40 % compared with P0 group separately. By virtue of the inclusion of PDs, the production of C–S–H and Fs in cement pastes go through substantial elevation, thereby markedly amplifying the physical adsorption and chemical binding capacity of chloride in PDs-containing cement pastes.

SEM ( Fig. 9) is utilized to analyze the morphological characteristics of various phases within PDs-incorporated cement pastes following 14 days of immersion in NaCl solution. Generally, CH crystals typically manifest in a hexagonal sheet-like morphology with more than 3 μm of dimension, and Fs crystals exhibit a similar hexagonal, flaky structure, yet are characterized by a dimension of 1–3 μm. Moreover, amorphous C–S–H tends to exhibit an irregular fibrous shape or granular shape [38]. As shown in Fig. 9a, numerous thin, hexagonally-shaped Fs crystals are haphazardly dispersed, alongside a smaller quantity of thicker CH sheets. Meanwhile, a quantity of C–S–H randomly adhere to Fs and CH. In the case of P1 and P2 groups (containing 0.025 wt% and 0.05 wt% PDs, respectively) shown in Fig. 9b and c, an increased content of C–S–H and Fs is observed, and these phases exhibit a more dense distribution. This implies that the addition of PDs elevates the generation and densification of C–S–H and Fs, thereby augmenting the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding. Moreover, a reduced occurrence of CH sheets is observed, potentially attributing to the cover of Fs and C–S–H. With reference to P3 and P4 groups (containing 0.1 wt% and 0.2 wt% PDs, respectively) in Fig. 9d and e, there is a further escalation in the content of Fs corresponding to the increasing of PDs dosage, aligning well with the findings of XRD and DTG. More significantly, as the addition of PDs increases, the distribution of Fs becomes more homogeneous and forms a petal-like structure. This is more pronounced in P4 group (Fig. 9e), further implying that PDs are able to enhance the generation of Fs and amplify their distribution from disorder to uniformly petal-like shape, finally improving the chloride chemical binding in paste samples. Moreover, a great bulk of C–S–H uniformly disperse and adhere to Fs crystals, thus remarkably enhancing the chloride ahysical adsorption. In consequence, the effects of PDs, encompassing promoting the generation and dense distribution of Fs and C–S–H, confer superior chloride immobilization on cementitious materials. This beneficial influence of PDs may also lead to the refinement of the pore structure within cement matrices, potentially enhancing chloride migration resistance within cement matrices.

It is widely recognized that the porosity of cement matrices is closely correlated with their chloride migration resistance [49]. Hence, MIP is employed to obtain comprehensive insight into the pore structures of diverse paste samples. As manifested in Fig. 10a, among all the groups, P0 group exhibits the largest cumulative pore volume. It tends to negatively affect the permeability of cement matrices. Regarding PDs-containing samples, their cumulative pore volume gradually decreases as the dosage of PDs increases, and P4 group with 0.2 wt% PDs possesses the smallest cumulative pore volume. This substantiates that the incorporation of PDs notably diminishes the cumulative pore volume of cement matrices, probably attributing to the PDs-promoted generation and densification of hydration products, thereby refining the pore structure of cement matrices. This is predominately responsible for the lower W a values of PDs-containing paste samples. Fig. 10b showcases the pore size distributions of various samples. Compared with P0 group, it is clear that the pore volume of the PDs-modified samples markedly decreases across all pore size ranges, and P4 group consistently owns the smallest pore volume irrespective of pore diameters. This further confirms that by virtue of their hydration promotion, PDs contribute to the refinement of the pore structures within cement matrices, resulting in significantly reduced pore volumes.

According to the previous study [50], the pores of cementitious materials can be divided into four distinct types: harmless pores measuring less than 20 nm, less harmful pores ranging from 20 to 100 nm, harmful pores spanning 100–200 nm, and more harmful pores exceeding 200 nm. Table 5 illustrates the total porosity and pore size classification of diverse samples. Apparently, the total porosity of PDs-incorporated groups is less than that of the P0 group (24.04 %), and higher dosages of PDs result in lower porosity of cement matrices. P4 group presents the lowest total porosity (17.44 %), followed by P3 group (21.43 %), P2 group (21.53 %), and P1 group (21.81 %). This further indicates that the induction of PDs dramatically refines the microstructure of cement matrices by boosting the formation and densification of hydration products. More significantly, there is a visible augment in the proportion of harmless pores in PDs-containing samples, and the proportion of harmful pores correspondingly diminished. Among them, P4 group has the lowest proportion of more harmful pores (34.6 %) and the highest proportion of harmless pores (22.45 %), decreased by 22 % and increased by 34 % than P0 group, respectively. This reaffirms that PDs-modified samples exhibit a more refined pore structure, thus effectively impeding the chloride migration resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PDs enhancing chloride immobilization of cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: Following the extensive phase composition and microstructure analyses previously conducted, a plausible mechanism for chloride immobilization of cement pastes modified with PDs is brought forward and schematically illustrated in Fig. 11. That is, incorporating PDs facilitates the generation and compactification of C–S–H and Fs, thus drastically amplifying the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding within cement pastes. Meanwhile, the porosity of cement matrices is dramatically refined owing to the PDs-promoted generation of hydration products, thereby effectively enhancing their migration resistance within cement matrices. More specifically, in the absence of PDs, cement hydration is insufficient, giving rise to the shortage of C–S–H. Simultaneously, there is an inadequate number of AFm phases to transform into Fs. Consequently, the chloride immobilization abbility of paste samples is weak due to inferior chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding. Additionally, due to the deficient quantity of hydration products (mainly C–S–H), the internal structure of the cement matrices is loose and porous, making free chloride ions easily permeate the cement matrices through the pore solution. At this time, cement-based materials are highly susceptible to chloride-induced corrosion. As for PDs, they possess identical characteristics as other nanomaterials (e.g. GO), namely, nucleation effect. In other words, PDs boost the nucleation of hydration products to facilitate the hydration process in cementitious materials. Thus, multitudes of Fs (formed by ion exchange between AFm phase and chloride ions) and C–S–H are generated and tend to densify (determined by XRD, DTG, and SEM), bringing about a staggering enhancement effect on both physical adsorption and chemical binding. Correspondingly, due to PDs-caused hydration products increase, the pore structure within cement matrices is significantly refined, thereby effectively reducing the pore volume (certified by MIP) and eventually amplifying the chloride migration resistance of cement matrices. More importantly, PDs exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology (shown in TEM) with high dispersity and own numerous oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups (demonstrated by FTIR and XPS). This not only contributes to further amplifying their dispersity in cement paste, but also remarkably elevates cement hydration, thus endowing PDs with outstanding improvement of chloride immobilization in cement pastes. Meanwhile, PDs present a mean particle size of ca. 19.89 nm (statistically by TEM), allowing them to fill the pore structure of cement matrices to a certain degree, thus substantially reducing the ratio of more harmful pores and increasing the ratio of harmless pores (calculated by MIP). As a result, the W a values of PDs-containing samples are greatly reduced, thereby weakening the transportation of free chloride ions within cement matrices. All in all, the mechanism of PDs improving chloride immobilization from cement pastes is rationally attributed to the synergetic effect of PDs, namely enhancing the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding within cement pastes, as well as improving the chloride migration resistance of cement matrices.

In conclusion, to improve the anti-chloride-corrosion capacity of RC structures, highly dispersed and cost-efficient PDs serve as a newfangled and eco-friendly nano-additive and are utilized for the first time to enhance the chloride immobilization of cement composites. Meanwhile, on account of phase compositions and microstructure analyses, the mechanism of PDs-promoted chloride immobilization of cementitious materials is reasonably put forward. The main findings are shown below:
(1)
Novel and eco-friendly PDs are successfully synthesized by a facile approach at ambient temperature based on Schiff reaction between p-BQ and o-PD. The as-prepared PDs exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of ca. 19.89 nm, low crystallinity, plenty of oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups, and distinct optical properties. More significantly, PDs demonstrate exceptional long-term dispersion stability in saturated CH solution (i.e. simulated concrete pore solution), enabling them to fulfill the satisfaction for applications in cement composites.
(2)
PDs effectively enhance the chloride immobilization performance of cement pastes following immersion in NaCl solution for 14 d. Furthermore, PDs considerably reduce the W a values of hardened cement pastes following curing for 28 d. Specifically, the C I values of paste samples progressively elevate, and their W a values accordingly decrease with the dosage of PDs from 0 to 0.2 wt%. When the dosage of PDs is 0.2 wt%, the C I value is significantly boosted by 106 % compared with the reference group, and the W a value is decreased by 11.5 %.
(3)
XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP demonstrated that PDs substantially facilitate the formation and densification of Fs and C–S–H, with higher PDs dosage leading to more Fs and C–S–H generation. Meanwhile, PDs also promote the refinement of pore volume within cement matrices, reducing the ratio of more harmful pores by 22 % and increasing the harmless pores by 34 % compared with the reference group.
(4)
Based on the analyses of the phase composition and microstructure of PDs-containing cement pastes, the relevant mechanism of PDs enhancing chloride immobilization within cement pastes is plausibly attributed to the synergetic improvement of the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding within cement paste, coupled with the augmentation of chloride migration resistance of cement matrices. For one thing, PDs facilitate the formation of C–S–H and Fs, bringing about a staggering enhancement of the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding. For another thing, PDs-induced hydration products refine pore structure, dramatically elevating chloride migration resistance within cement matrices.

These observations highlight the enormous potential of PDs in augmenting the chloride corrosion resistance of cementitious materials, thus providing a novel, cost-effective, and high-dispersibility nanomaterial to develop advanced cement-based materials with excellent durability. This is envisaged to bolster the longevity of RC structures under challenging coastal environments and deicing salt conditions.

Wu-Jian Long: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hong-Ru Guo: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Chuang He: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. An-Nan Zhong: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shu-Yi Zheng: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), and the National Natural Science Foundations of China, NSFC-Shandong Joint Fund (No. U2006223). The authors acknowledge the Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for conducting the XPS measurements.

Zhang, Carbon Dots as a Potential Therapeutic Agent for the Treatment of Cancer-Related Anemia, Adv. Mater. 34(19) (2022) 220905.
